questions.question: Explain the purpose of RelativeLayout in Android and list any four attributes used for positioning child views.
questions.hint: Remember that RelativeLayout allows relative positioning using IDs of other views
keyPoints: RelativeLayout is a view group that positions child views relative to each other or to the parent
android:layout_below - positions view below another view
android:layout_toRightOf - positions view to the right of another view
android:layout_alignParentTop - aligns view with parent's top edge
android:layout_centerInParent - centers view in parent container
android:layout_above - positions view above another view

questions.question: What is TableLayout in Android? Explain the purpose of TableRow and mention two important attributes of TableLayout.
questions.hint: Think of TableLayout as an HTML table structure with rows and cells
keyPoints: TableLayout arranges its children in rows and columns
TableRow represents a single row in the table
Each TableRow automatically creates columns for each child view
android:stretchColumns - specifies which columns can be stretched
android:shrinkColumns - allows columns to shrink
android:collapseColumns - hides columns from display

questions.question: Differentiate between android:layout_weight and android:gravity attributes in Android layouts.
questions.hint: Remember: layout_weight is about the view's size distribution, gravity is about content alignment
keyPoints: android:layout_weight distributes extra space in the parent among children
android:gravity positions content inside the view itself
layout_weight requires the dimension (width/height) to be set to 0dp for proper calculation
gravity affects alignment of content within the view boundaries
Weight is used primarily in LinearLayout, not RelativeLayout

questions.question: Write an XML code snippet demonstrating a RelativeLayout with three buttons: Button A at top-left, Button B below Button A, and Button C to the right of Button B.
questions.hint: Use android:layout_below and android:layout_toRightOf attributes with view IDs
keyPoints: Root RelativeLayout with match_parent dimensions
Button A with layout_alignParentTop=true and layout_alignParentStart=true
Button B with layout_below="@+id/buttonA"
Button C with layout_below="@+id/buttonA" and layout_toRightOf="@+id/buttonB"
Proper use of android:id and android:layout_width/layout_height

questions.question: Explain the android:stretchColumns attribute in TableLayout with an example.
questions.hint: Think of stretchColumns as making table columns fill the remaining width
keyPoints: android:stretchColumns specifies which columns can expand to fill extra horizontal space
Column indices are comma-separated (0,1,2) or use asterisk (*) for all columns
Example: android:stretchColumns="1,2" makes columns 1 and 2 stretchable
Example: android:stretchColumns="*" makes all columns stretch equally
This is useful for utilizing available screen width efficiently

questions.question: List and explain three alignment attributes available in RelativeLayout for positioning views relative to the parent container.
questions.hint: These attributes start with 'alignParent' and reference the parent container
keyPoints: android:layout_alignParentTop - aligns top edge with parent's top
android:layout_alignParentBottom - aligns bottom edge with parent's bottom
android:layout_alignParentStart - aligns start edge with parent's start (left in LTR)
android:layout_alignParentEnd - aligns end edge with parent's end (right in LTR)
android:layout_centerInParent - centers both vertically and horizontally
android:layout_centerHorizontal - centers only horizontally
android:layout_centerVertical - centers only vertically

questions.question: Design a complete login screen layout using RelativeLayout. The screen should contain: a logo/image at the top-center, username EditText below the logo, password EditText below username, Login Button below password, and Register TextView at the bottom-center. Write the XML layout code with appropriate attributes.
questions.hint: Plan the vertical chain of views first, then add horizontal centering
keyPoints: Root RelativeLayout with match_parent for width and height
ImageView/TextView for logo with layout_centerHorizontal=true and layout_alignParentTop=true
EditText for username with layout_below logo and layout_centerHorizontal=true
EditText for password with layout_below username and layout_centerHorizontal=true
Button for login with layout_below password and layout_centerHorizontal=true
TextView for register with layout_alignParentBottom=true and layout_centerHorizontal=true
Proper use of margins, padding, and IDs for all views

questions.question: Create a TableLayout to display a student marks table with three columns: Subject, Marks, and Grade. Include at least 4 rows of data. Explain the attributes used to make the table presentable.
questions.hint: First row is typically the header, followed by data rows
keyPoints: TableLayout with android:stretchColumns="*" to fill width
Header row with TableRow containing TextViews for column titles
android:textStyle="bold" for header row
4 data rows with TableRow containing marks data
android:padding for cell spacing
android:gravity="center" for centering text in cells
Use of proper IDs and layout_width="match_parent"

questions.question: Compare RelativeLayout and TableLayout in Android under the following headings: (a) Use cases (b) Performance (c) Flexibility (d) Complexity.
questions.hint: Consider practical scenarios where each layout is preferred
keyPoints: Use cases: RelativeLayout - complex positioning, forms, complex UIs; TableLayout - structured data, grids, tables
Performance: RelativeLayout performs more layout passes (measure-pass twice); TableLayout simpler structure
Complexity: RelativeLayout requires understanding of relative positioning; TableLayout more intuitive for tabular data
Flexibility: RelativeLayout - highly flexible, any arrangement; TableLayout - rigid row-column structure
Nesting: Both can be nested, but deep nesting affects performance

questions.question: Explain the following RelativeLayout attributes with examples: (a) android:layout_toStartOf (b) android:layout_toEndOf (c) android:layout_alignBaseline (d) android:layout_marginStart.
questions.hint: Remember to use android:layout_marginStart instead of marginLeft for better RTL support
keyPoints: android:layout_toStartOf - positions view to start (left) of referenced view, example: layout_toStartOf="@+id/viewId"
android:layout_toEndOf - positions view to end (right) of referenced view
android:layout_alignBaseline - aligns text baseline with another view's baseline
android:layout_marginStart - adds space at the start (left) edge of the view
These attributes help create precise relative positions between views

questions.question: Design a registration form using TableLayout with the following fields: Name, Email, Phone, Address, and a Submit Button. Each field should have a label on the left and input field on the right. Explain the layout design decisions.
questions.hint: Use android:layout_span to make the button span multiple columns
keyPoints: TableLayout with android:stretchColumns="1" to make input columns expand
Each row contains two views: TextView (label) and EditText (input)
First column (labels) with fixed or wrap_content width
Second column (inputs) with match_parent to fill remaining space
Submit button in a row spanning both columns using android:layout_span="2"
Use of android:padding and android:gravity for proper alignment

questions.question: In a LinearLayout with horizontal orientation, you have three buttons with layout_width="0dp" and layout_weight values of 1, 2, and 3 respectively. If the total available width is 600dp, calculate the width each button will occupy. Show the calculation steps.
questions.hint: Width = (individual weight / total weight) * available width
keyPoints: Total weight = 1 + 2 + 3 = 6
Button 1 width = (1/6) * 600 = 100dp
Button 2 width = (2/6) * 600 = 200dp
Button 3 width = (3/6) * 600 = 300dp
Verification: 100 + 200 + 300 = 600dp

questions.question: Given a TableLayout with android:stretchColumns="0,2" and three columns in total, if the screen width is 360dp and the first and third columns take 80dp each for their content, calculate the width of the middle column. Explain the stretching behavior.
questions.hint: Only stretchColumns (0 and 2) can expand; column 1 gets only its content width
keyPoints: Content width of column 0 = 80dp
Content width of column 2 = 80dp
Total content width = 80 + 80 = 160dp
Available space for stretching = 360 - 160 = 200dp
Middle column (non-stretchable) gets its content width
Columns 0 and 2 will stretch equally to fill more space if available

questions.question: Consider a RelativeLayout with a parent width of 320dp. A child Button A is positioned with android:layout_alignParentStart="true" and android:layout_width="100dp". Another Button B is positioned with android:layout_toRightOf="@+id/buttonA". Calculate the maximum possible width of Button B. What attribute would you add to make Button B use the remaining space?
questions.hint: Subtract Button A's width from parent width to find remaining space
keyPoints: Parent width = 320dp
Button A width = 100dp
Maximum Button B width = 320 - 100 = 220dp
To use remaining space, add android:layout_width="match_parent" to Button B
Alternatively, use android:layout_toEndOf with match_parent
The calculation assumes no margins are set on either button

questions.question: A TableLayout has android:shrinkColumns="1" and android:stretchColumns="2". Column 0 has fixed content of 50dp, Column 1 has content of 150dp, and Column 2 has content of 100dp. If the screen width is 400dp, calculate the final display width of each column after shrinking and stretching is applied.
questions.hint: Shrinking reduces column width, stretching increases column width to fill space
keyPoints: Total content width = 50 + 150 + 100 = 300dp
Available extra space = 400 - 300 = 100dp
Column 1 shrinks (shrinkColumns="1") - loses excess width proportionally
Column 2 stretches (stretchColumns="2") - gains extra width
Column 0 fixed at 50dp (neither shrinks nor stretches)
Final: Column 0 = 50dp, Column 1 < 150dp, Column 2 > 100dp
